claims.claim: For an AI-native OS-layer, the big three's 63% compute concentration plus per-GB egress tax make hyperscaler-native architecture a liability to route around, not a platform to adopt. The cheapest robust posture is local-first inference + data residency, with cheap cloud APIs ($0.10/1M input tier) as burst capacity and frontier cloud reserved for hard reasoning - exactly inverting the default startup instinct to build cloud-native and optimise later.
claims.why_distinctive: Inverts the conventional cloud-native default: treats hyperscaler gravity (concentration + egress lock-in) as the thing the wedge exploits, and frames local-first as the cost-optimal AND sovereignty-optimal base case rather than a niche privacy play.
claims.falsifier: A grounded TCO for our actual workload shows cloud-native total cost (including egress and frontier calls) beating local-first + burst by a clear margin at our realistic volume.
claims.verify: Build the wm-ad-hybrid-router cost model with live pecollective/provider rates + measured on-device $/token for a baseline device, then run our projected token mix through it and compare against an all-cloud baseline.

human_input.note: Director would likely frame this as: 'we are a plugin to the internet, not a tenant of it - the cloud is a metered utility we arbitrage, and owning the data-residency boundary is the point, with cost parity a bonus not the driver.'

claims.claim: Open-weight cloud APIs at ~$1.97/1M tokens undercutting both frontier cloud and owned hardware ($7.15/1M heavy-tier) will, by ~2027, kill the 'own GPUs to save money' case for all but privacy-, latency-, or sovereignty-bound workloads. The durable reason to run on-device will be control, not price - so our on-device story must be sold on sovereignty, never on cost.
claims.why_distinctive: Most local-LLM advocacy leans on a cost argument; this claims the cost argument is evaporating under open-weight API competition and the ~10x/yr decline, and that betting our wedge's on-device pitch on price is a trap.
claims.falsifier: Open-weight API pricing stalls or rises while frontier-capability open weights stop appearing, so owned hardware remains the cheapest path to capable inference past 2027.
claims.verify: Track open-weight hosted API $/1M (e.g. Together, Fireworks, DeepInfra) vs owned-hardware amortised $/1M quarterly through 2026-2027; watch whether the gap holds at ~3-4x.

human_input.note: Director would likely qualify: 'agreed price is a losing pitch - but on-device is non-negotiable for us because the tape and user data must never leave the boundary; we run local because sovereignty is the product, and we let the cheap APIs do the commodity work.'

claims.claim: The next compute moat is not price but bundling at the inference layer: Apple (Private Cloud Compute + on-device foundation models) and Google (Gemini Nano in-OS) can subsidise first-party local inference while metering or rate-limiting third-party access to the same NPU/PCC, recreating the app-store gate one layer down. An OS-layer that depends on platform-provided on-device models inherits a new chokepoint.
claims.why_distinctive: Reframes 'on-device AI' from a democratising force into a potential new gatekeeping surface, and warns that our hybrid router must ship its own bundled weights rather than lean on Apple/Google foundation-model APIs.
claims.falsifier: Apple and Google expose on-device/PCC foundation-model inference to third-party apps on equal, unmetered terms with first-party apps and keep it that way.
claims.verify: Read Apple Foundation Models framework + Private Cloud Compute terms and Android AICore/Gemini Nano access terms for third-party rate limits, attestation requirements, and first-party carve-outs.

human_input.note: Director would likely frame: 'this is the app-store fight again - so we carry our own model weights and our own router; never let the inference primitive be something Apple or Google can throttle. Independence at the model layer is the same sovereignty principle as independence at the distribution layer.'
